chunk_summary: Therapeutic feeding in infants under six months with SAM focuses on restoring breastfeeding using techniques like SST or SNS, while older children require phased feeding protocols with F-75 and F-100 formulas, micronutrient supplementation, and careful monitoring for complications.
learning_objectives: Understand therapeutic feeding protocols for infants under six months with severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Learn phased feeding strategies for children 6–24 months with SAM, including the use of F-75 and F-100 formulas.
Identify the role of micronutrient supplementation and rehydration protocols in managing SAM.
Explore relactation strategies such as the supplemental suckling technique (SST) and supplemental nursing systems (SNS).
key_facts: For infants under six months with SAM, prioritize breastfeeding restoration using SST or SNS to stimulate milk production.
In children 6–24 months with SAM, stabilization involves F-75 formula in small, frequent feeds to prevent heart failure and refeeding syndrome.
Transition to F-100 or energy-dense local foods during the rehabilitation phase to achieve catch-up growth.
Micronutrient supplementation includes vitamin A, zinc, multivitamins, and delayed iron until stabilization.
Rehydration in SAM requires specific protocols like ReSoMal to avoid complications.
Uncomplicated SAM with good appetite can be managed outpatient with ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF).
reference_quote: For severe acute malnutrition, feeding is medical therapy and must follow phased physiology. In infants <6 months, prioritize restoring effective breastfeeding rather than long-term formula dependence. (Source: WHO Guideline updates on the management of severe acute malnutrition in infants and children)
document_key_topics: Therapeutic feeding in severe acute malnutrition (SAM)
Relactation strategies for infants under six months
Phased feeding protocols for children 6–24 months with SAM
Micronutrient supplementation and rehydration in malnourished children
